text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Assessment and New Patient Consultation

**PATIENT:** Margaret Yuen
**AGE:** 62 years
**REFERRING PHYSICIAN:** Primary care medicine
**REASON FOR CONSULTATION:** New diagnosis of right upper lobe lung mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy, EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma. Multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) review and treatment planning.

---

**CHIEF COMPLAINT:**
Ms. Yuen is a 62-year-old Chinese-American retired high school chemistry teacher who presents to thoracic oncology for evaluation of a newly diagnosed Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma. She was referred following CT-guided biopsy of the primary mass and endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy of ipsilateral mediastinal lymph nodes, both confirming metastatic adenocarcinoma with an EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:**
Ms. Yuen initially presented to her primary care physician approximately 8 weeks ago with a 3-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea, a persistent dry cough, and mild right-sided chest discomfort. She denied hemoptysis, significant weight loss, or fever. Given her smoking history and symptom trajectory, she underwent a chest radiograph which demonstrated a right upper lobe opacity. Subsequent CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed a 4.8 cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe with enlarged right paratracheal and subcarinal lymph nodes. She was referred to interventional pulmonology for tissue acquisition.

CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right upper lobe mass and concurrent EBUS-guided fine needle aspiration of the right paratracheal lymph node (station 4R) were performed. Pathology confirmed invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma, acinar-predominant pattern, TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A positive, consistent with primary pulmonary origin. Lymph node FNA confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma, confirming N2 nodal involvement. Molecular testing reflexed to the laboratory showed EGFR L858R mutation in exon 21 by cobas PCR, confirmed on comprehensive NGS (500-gene panel; VAF 42%). PD-L1 TPS was 15% by 22C3 assay. ALK and ROS1 were negative by IHC and FISH, respectively.

Whole-body PET-CT demonstrated a hypermetabolic 4.8 cm right upper lobe mass (SUVmax 14.2) with hypermetabolic right paratracheal (station 4R, SUVmax 8.7) and subcarinal (station 7, SUVmax 6.4) lymphadenopathy, consistent with multi-station N2 disease. No distant metastatic lesions were identified on PET-CT. Dedicated MRI brain with gadolinium showed no intracranial metastases. Comprehensive NGS also identified TP53 R248W co-mutation (VAF 38%), STK11 wild-type, KEAP1 wild-type, and no co-occurring resistance mutations. Tumor mutational burden was low at 6.2 mutations/megabase; MSI was stable. No fusions were detected. The overall clinical staging is consistent with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0, AJCC 8th edition) right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma.

Ms. Yuen was presented at the thoracic multidisciplinary tumor board today, with attendance from thoracic surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology (this service), interventional pulmonology, radiology, and pathology.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:**
- Constitutional: Fatigue, mild -- present. No significant unintentional weight loss (approximately 2 kg over 3 months). No fever, chills, or night sweats.
- Pulmonary: Persistent dry cough, present for approximately 3 months. Exertional dyspnea, worsened over the past 6-8 weeks. She reports that her baseline exercise tolerance has decreased from being able to walk 4-6 blocks to approximately 1-2 blocks. She uses albuterol as needed for her COPD approximately 2-3 times per week. No hemoptysis. No pleuritic chest pain.
- Neurologic: No headache, no focal weakness, no vision changes, no cognitive changes. No seizures.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain or new joint complaints.
- Gastrointestinal: Appetite mildly reduced. No nausea, vomiting, dysphagia, or change in bowel habits.
- Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms.
- Integumentary: No new skin lesions or rashes.
- Endocrine: No polyuria or polydipsia beyond her known diabetes baseline. HbA1c checked recently at 7.1% (per PCP records).

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:**
1. Hypertension -- well-controlled on antihypertensive therapy
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled, HbA1c approximately 7.1% at most recent check
3. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), GOLD Stage I -- mild; on albuterol PRN; no prior hospitalizations for COPD exacerbations
4. Former smoker -- 20 pack-year history; quit approximately 12 years prior to current diagnosis (approximately age 50)
5. No prior history of malignancy
6. No prior thoracic surgeries or procedures

**SURGICAL HISTORY:**
- Cholecystectomy (laparoscopic), approximately 15 years ago, uncomplicated
- No prior thoracic surgeries

**FAMILY HISTORY:**
- Mother: Deceased, gastric cancer at age 71
- Father: Deceased, ischemic heart disease at age 78
- One sibling (brother): Living, hypertension; no known malignancy
- No known first-degree relatives with lung cancer, colorectal cancer, or breast/ovarian cancer
- No known hereditary cancer syndromes in the family

**SOCIAL HISTORY:**
- Retired high school chemistry teacher; previously employed in a laboratory and classroom setting for approximately 30 years. No known significant occupational exposure to asbestos, radon, or other lung carcinogens beyond cigarette smoke
- Former smoker: 20 pack-years; quit approximately 12 years ago
- No current alcohol use (reports occasional social use, less than 1-2 drinks per week)
- No illicit drug use
- Lives at home with her husband in San Francisco, CA; two adult children (a son and daughter), both local
- Socially active prior to current illness onset; describes significant decline in activity tolerance over the past 2-3 months
- Has medical insurance; able to attend frequent appointments
- Speaks English fluently; Cantonese spoken at home
- Strong family support system; daughter attends appointments when possible

**ALLERGIES:**
- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies
- NKDA documented in chart

**CURRENT MEDICATIONS:**
1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
3. Albuterol (salbutamol) 90 mcg/actuation MDI, 2 puffs inhaled PRN (COPD, as needed)
4. Tiotropium (Spiriva) 18 mcg inhaled once daily (COPD maintenance)
5. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis)
6. Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily (hyperlipidemia, per PCP records)
7. Vitamin D3 1000 IU orally once daily

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:**
- General: Well-appearing, well-nourished Chinese-American woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and engaged. ECOG Performance Status: 1.
- Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 95% on room air, temperature 36.8°C, weight 62 kg, height 160 cm, BMI 24.2 kg/m2.
- HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. Oropharynx clear, no lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular chains (no palpable supraclavicular adenopathy bilaterally).
- Lymphatic: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy in axillary or inguinal regions.
- Pulmonary: Mildly decreased breath sounds at the right upper lobe. No wheezing or stridor. Mild end-expiratory rhonchi bilaterally consistent with known COPD. No dullness to percussion suggesting pleural effusion. Mild, non-productive cough elicited during examination.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No S3 or S4 gallop. No murmurs. No jugular venous distension. No peripheral edema.
- Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present and active. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. Well-healed right subcostal scar from prior cholecystectomy.
- Musculoskeletal: No focal bone tenderness on palpation of the spine, ribs, or long bones. Full range of motion in all extremities. No digit clubbing.
- Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal. No Horner's syndrome.
- Skin: No suspicious skin lesions.

---

**LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS (reviewed at time of consultation):**

Complete Blood Count (CBC):
- WBC: 7.4 x10^9/L (differential: neutrophils 65%, lymphocytes 24%, monocytes 8%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 1%)
- Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL (mildly low; likely related to chronic disease; no active bleeding)
- Hematocrit: 38.2%
- Platelets: 284 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP):
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
- BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Glucose 118 mg/dL (non-fasting)
- Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L
- Total protein 7.0 g/dL, Albumin 3.9 g/dL
- Calcium 9.2 mg/dL
- LDH: 198 U/L (within institutional normal range)

HbA1c: 7.1% (diabetes moderately controlled)

Coagulation: PT/INR and aPTT within normal limits

**Imaging Results (reviewed):**
- PET-CT (whole body): 4.8 cm hypermetabolic right upper lobe mass (SUVmax 14.2) with spiculated margins. Hypermetabolic right paratracheal (4R, SUVmax 8.7) and subcarinal (station 7, SUVmax 6.4) lymphadenopathy. No evidence of distant metastases. No pleural effusion.
- MRI Brain with gadolinium: No intracranial metastases. Normal brain parenchyma. No leptomeningeal enhancement.
- Pulmonary function tests (reviewed from prior PCP records; formal PFTs performed approximately 18 months ago): FEV1 80% predicted, FEV1/FVC ratio 0.68 -- consistent with mild obstructive pattern (COPD GOLD Stage I).

**Pathology (reviewed with pathologist at MDT):**
- Right upper lobe core biopsy: Invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma, acinar-predominant, TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A positive, p40 negative
- Station 4R EBUS FNA: Metastatic adenocarcinoma
- EGFR L858R (exon 21) detected -- sensitizing mutation; ALK negative; ROS1 negative
- PD-L1 TPS: 15% (22C3 assay)

**NGS (reviewed):**
- EGFR L858R (VAF 42%), TP53 R248W (VAF 38%)
- STK11 WT, KEAP1 WT -- favorable co-mutation profile from a TKI-response standpoint
- No fusions, no T790M, no exon 20 insertion
- TMB low (6.2 mut/Mb); MSS

---

**MDT DISCUSSION SUMMARY:**

Ms. Yuen was formally presented at the thoracic multidisciplinary tumor board with attendance from thoracic surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, interventional pulmonology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.

Thoracic Surgery Assessment:
Dr. [Thoracic Surgery] reviewed the imaging in detail. Given the CT and PET-CT findings demonstrating multi-station ipsilateral mediastinal N2 disease (stations 4R and 7, both with FDG-avidity confirmed on PET and histologically confirmed at station 4R via EBUS), the tumor was deemed surgically unresectable at this time. Thoracic surgery deferred a surgical recommendation and will reassess if downstaging is achieved with systemic or combined modality therapy.

Radiation Oncology Assessment:
Dr. [Radiation Oncology] discussed the role of definitive concurrent chemoradiation (CRT) in unresectable Stage III NSCLC, which has historically been the standard of care, followed by durvalumab consolidation per the PACIFIC trial paradigm. However, the team acknowledged that this patient has an actionable EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation, which substantially alters the treatment decision landscape. It was noted that in EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC, the role of consolidation durvalumab post-CRT is less clear (the PACIFIC trial largely did not include patients with EGFR mutations in the analysis supporting durvalumab benefit), and the LAURA trial has demonstrated a significant progression-free survival benefit with osimertinib consolidation following definitive chemoradiation in EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC. Radiation oncology expressed willingness to plan definitive CRT to total doses of approximately 60-66 Gy with concurrent platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin/etoposide or carboplatin/paclitaxel), followed by osimertinib maintenance.

Medical Oncology Assessment (this service):
Given the pathologically confirmed EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation (a third-generation EGFR TKI sensitizing alteration), osimertinib (Tagrisso, 80 mg orally daily) is the systemic agent of choice. Two potential approaches were discussed:
1. Definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by osimertinib consolidation/maintenance (LAURA trial-based approach, FDA-approved indication)
2. Upfront osimertinib monotherapy without concurrent chemoradiation, given that osimertinib achieves excellent systemic disease control in EGFR-sensitizing mutation-positive NSCLC

The favorable molecular profile (STK11 WT, KEAP1 WT) supports robust TKI response. The low PD-L1 TPS of 15% does not argue strongly for immunotherapy monotherapy, and given the EGFR mutation, anti-PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy would not be recommended as initial treatment per standard guidelines. The co-occurring TP53 R248W mutation is of interest; TP53 co-mutations in EGFR-mutant NSCLC can be associated with modestly shorter response duration, but this does not change the initial treatment recommendation.

Patient Preference Discussion:
Following MDT deliberation, the treatment recommendation was presented to Ms. Yuen in detail with her daughter in attendance. I spent approximately 45 minutes discussing:
- The diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma
- The unresectable nature of the disease due to multi-station N2 involvement
- The standard CRT followed by osimertinib approach (LAURA trial data)
- The role of upfront osimertinib monotherapy as an alternative
- The toxicity profile of concurrent chemoradiation, including risk of radiation pneumonitis (particularly relevant given her underlying COPD, even mild), esophagitis, fatigue, cytopenias, and potential long-term pulmonary fibrosis
- The toxicity profile of osimertinib monotherapy: primarily dermatologic (acneiform rash), GI (diarrhea), and paronychia; rare but serious risks including interstitial lung disease (ILD) and QTc prolongation
- The curability discussion: Stage IIIB disease is potentially curable with combined modality therapy; however, approximately 30-40% of patients with Stage III NSCLC achieve long-term disease control with current approaches

Ms. Yuen expressed significant concern regarding the toxicity of concurrent chemoradiation, particularly given her underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) and fear of radiation-related pulmonary complications. She noted that her COPD, while mild, has already impacted her quality of life. After extensive shared decision-making and after being given time to discuss with her family, she declined concurrent chemoradiation and elected to proceed with upfront osimertinib monotherapy.

This decision was respected. We discussed that while the LAURA data supports CRT-then-osimertinib in this setting, the evidence base for upfront osimertinib in Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC is also supported by retrospective and single-arm prospective data, and that osimertinib monotherapy has demonstrated high response rates in this molecular subset. We will monitor closely with regular cross-sectional imaging and will revisit the role of thoracic radiation if there is disease stabilization or response.

---

**ASSESSMENT:**

1. Stage IIIB (T3N2M0, AJCC 8th edition) right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant (exon 21 L858R, VAF 42%), PD-L1 TPS 15%, ALK-negative, ROS1-negative. Multi-station N2 disease (stations 4R and 7), unresectable per thoracic surgery. No evidence of distant metastases.

2. Underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) -- mild, on tiotropium and albuterol PRN. FEV1 80% predicted. Patient's concern regarding CRT-related pulmonary toxicity in the context of underlying COPD is clinically reasonable and was a significant factor in treatment decision-making.

3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled (HbA1c 7.1%); will require monitoring during treatment (corticosteroid use for any TKI toxicity management may transiently worsen glycemic control).

4. Hypertension -- well-controlled on lisinopril.

5. Mild anemia (Hgb 12.8 g/dL) -- likely anemia of chronic disease/inflammation; will monitor on treatment.

---

**PLAN:**

1. Initiate osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line treatment for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma. Target start date: approximately 2 weeks from today to allow for treatment coordination, financial counseling, and patient preparation.

2. Prior to treatment initiation:
   - Baseline ECG to assess QTc interval (osimertinib can prolong QTc; baseline QTc reviewed today was 418 ms -- within normal limits)
   - Baseline ophthalmology evaluation given baseline dry eye history (patient reports occasional dry eyes at baseline)
   - Baseline echocardiogram given cardiac risk factors
   - Repeat LFTs, CBC, CMP prior to first dose
   - Formal pulmonary function testing (updated PFTs, as prior values are approximately 18 months old) to establish current baseline given COPD

3. Financial counseling: Facilitate access to AstraZeneca patient support program (Tagrisso ACCESS program) given osimertinib's significant cost. Patient has insurance but copay assistance should be explored.

4. Patient education:
   - Provided written educational materials on osimertinib side effects (acneiform rash, diarrhea, paronychia, fatigue, rare ILD)
   - Instructed to call our nurse triage line for any skin changes, dyspnea, or new neurologic symptoms
   - Advised to avoid concurrent CYP3A4 strong inducers (e.g., rifampin, St. John's Wort)
   - Sun protection counseling given risk of EGFR TKI dermatologic toxicity

5. Restaging imaging: Plan CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 12 weeks after initiation of osimertinib (first restaging scan). MRI brain to be repeated at approximately 6 months unless symptoms develop sooner.

6. Continue current medications: lisinopril, metformin, tiotropium, albuterol PRN, atorvastatin, aspirin, vitamin D3. No drug-drug interactions of concern with osimertinib anticipated.

7. Diabetes management: Coordinate with primary care physician regarding glycemic monitoring during treatment, particularly if corticosteroids are needed for any toxicity management.

8. Palliative care: Will refer to supportive care/palliative care team for symptom management (cough, dyspnea) and psychosocial support, consistent with ASCO guidelines recommending early integration of palliative care in advanced lung cancer.

9. Social work referral: Placed today for assistance with financial toxicity, caregiver support, and community resources.

10. Return to clinic: Approximately 4 weeks after initiating osimertinib for first on-treatment assessment (symptom review, labs, toxicity assessment). The patient and her daughter were given my direct contact information and nurse triage line.

**Prognosis discussed:** I had an honest and compassionate discussion with Ms. Yuen and her daughter regarding the nature of Stage IIIB NSCLC. While cure is our shared goal, I was transparent that Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC treated with EGFR TKI-based approaches has a favorable but heterogeneous trajectory. Median progression-free survival on osimertinib in Stage IV EGFR-mutant NSCLC is approximately 18-20 months (FLAURA data), and Stage III disease may behave differently. We discussed that the goal is long-term disease control and quality of life preservation. Psychosocial support and advance care planning discussions are available at any time.

**Cancer status: Present. ECOG Performance Status: 1.**

---

Dictated and reviewed by:
[Attending Thoracic Oncologist]
Thoracic Oncology Program
Time spent with patient: approximately 60 minutes, of which greater than 50% was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

CC: Primary care physician, radiation oncology, thoracic surgery
